pairs.question: Who was Cyril William 'Bill' Doody and what political offices did he hold in Canada?
pairs.answer: Cyril William 'Bill' Doody (1931–2005) was a Canadian politician from Newfoundland and Labrador. He served in the Newfoundland and Labrador House of Assembly from 1971 as a Progressive Conservative, becoming Minister of Mines, Agriculture and Resources in 1972, Finance Minister in 1975, and Minister of Mines and Energy in 1979. He later served in the Senate of Canada from 1979 to 2005, including as Deputy Leader of the Government in the Senate (1984–1991).

pairs.question: What significant political event occurred in March 1979 involving Bill Doody?
pairs.answer: In March 1979, Bill Doody was a candidate in the Progressive Conservative leadership convention to succeed Frank Moores as Premier of Newfoundland and Labrador. He was defeated by Brian Peckford in the convention held on March 17, 1979.

pairs.question: Why did Bill Doody leave provincial politics in 1979?
pairs.answer: Bill Doody left provincial politics in October 1979 after the 1979 Newfoundland election. He was appointed to the Senate of Canada by Prime Minister Joe Clark, marking his transition from provincial to federal politics.

pairs.question: What was Bill Doody's stance on the merger of the Progressive Conservatives and Canadian Alliance in 2004?
pairs.answer: When the Progressive Conservatives merged with the Canadian Alliance in December 2004 to form the Conservative Party of Canada, Bill Doody chose not to join the new party. Instead, he continued serving in the Senate as part of a five-person Progressive Conservative caucus that included Norman Atkins, Lowell Murray, Elaine McCoy, and Nancy Ruth.

pairs.question: What leadership role did Bill Doody hold in the Senate?
pairs.answer: Bill Doody served as the Deputy Leader of the Government in the Senate from September 17, 1984, until 1991. This position involved supporting the government's agenda and managing Senate proceedings on its behalf.

pairs.question: How did Bill Doody's political career begin before entering the House of Assembly?
pairs.answer: Before entering politics, Bill Doody worked as the managing director of a supermarket. In 1971, he resigned from this position to run for office, successfully winning a seat in the Newfoundland and Labrador House of Assembly as a Progressive Conservative.

pairs.question: What was Bill Doody's educational background?
pairs.answer: Bill Doody was educated at Saint Bonaventure's College in St. John's, Newfoundland. This institution played a role in his early life before he transitioned into a career in politics and business.
